The main problem with Fire and Ash is that it doesn’t feel like the story is moving forward or offering anything new. Instead, it feels like a direct continuation of The Way of Water arc, essentially completing that storyline with the same “the family is our fortress” theme.
Rather than calling it Fire and Ash, it should have been titled The Way of Water: Vol. 2. The name Fire and Ash led people to expect a new chapter and something fresh in this world, which ultimately created expectations the film didn’t meet.

Just watched the film. I’m glad I didn’t watch this trailer. Shows waaayyyy too much. But then again, most movies do nowadays instead of just sticking to one trailer. Overall I enjoyed the movie. Absolutely gorgeous, and the pacing was quite good considering it’s over 3 hours. Still feels long, but not a slog nor does it feel like a chore to get through. Yes there were a lot of the same story beats from the first movie, but a lot of it was pulled off in a way that felt it was justified. I enjoyed Varang and think she was a good villain and the Ash People as a whole a good addition to the franchise.

Just watched Avatar 3 and it was truly stunning. ✨
A beautiful film, fully living up to the high expectations set by its visionary director, James Cameron.

It absolutely earns a 10/10 and stands proudly alongside its predecessor, without losing its soul, depth, or magic. The world-building, visuals, and emotional weight pull you in from the very first moment and never let go.

Once again, Cameron proves why he’s a master of cinema: vision, detail, and an immersive experience that you don’t just watch — you feel.
If you love films that transport you to another world, Avatar 3 is not just a sequel, it’s a promise fulfilled. 🎬🌊

12/18/2025 - Avatar Fire and Ash releases tomorrow. It came out early today and I would love to see it right now but I’m waiting for the big day. I have been waiting for this day for so long. I remember counting the days, liking those posts on instagram that would countdown (day678). And now we're here. It releases tomorrow and I've watched the trailers many many times. I’m so eager and excited and I know it will be a masterpiece. No one else but James Cameron can do something like this, no one would do it better. So much has changed and happened since the second movie
Some things that have happened since the second - 
- Went to Michigan, Missouri, Toronto, Chicago, Indiana, Iowa, Nebraska, Colorado Denver, and Utah 🌁
- Went to Arizona again
- Got a gf, it's interesting right now and spicy. Lot of juicy lore hehe
- In college studying health sciences
- Just finished my second semester, got an A in anatomy and physiology 151
- Figured out I wanna be a Physician Assistant
- Became an emt 🚑
- Have 2000 PCE
- Started selling my own on Depop
- Got some money in my bank account
- Finally bought some of the shoes ive been wanting since high school (Vaeline ones, Winterized Loyals, Yeezy Waverunners, Union) 
- Learned to drive, ambulances even 
- Have many work stories, including a patient yelling in downtown san diego and police come to escort us
- Played more TLOU 2 and got really good at grounded (even played survival Perm and lasted 7 hours) 🧟‍♂
- Met patients that have impacted me (the dts who was a nurse and wanted to speak with me)
- Changed my hairstyle 
- Lost 50 lbs
- My music taste went from rap to rap, spanish, regatton, pop, indie, metal, blues, alternative rock, and some country
- Explored a lot of San diego and Riverside county in california
- Learned that I love science , particularly biology - courses like microbiology, a and p, genetics, virology, also courses in chemistry 🧬🔬

I watched Avatar 3 🔥 (Spoiler warning ⚠️)

I’m disappointed… I don’t like that they weren’t able to come up with a new villain in the third installment. I mean, they added Varang and her tribe, but it would have been better if they hadn’t brought humans back in this part, and if they had finally let Quaritch die for good. It would have been much more interesting if this installment focused only on fighting another tribe, because the whole “humans” storyline has already been seen and already sold. The first time, I was thrilled, but this time I was just disappointed because they weren’t able to come up with something new. It would have been amazing to watch a story without humans—two tribes fighting each other and using their territories and skills in that battle.

I don’t like what they did with the connection to the Tree of Souls, making it look like you’re putting on virtual reality goggles. (I mean, wtf was that?) It was always shown that when connecting to the Tree of Souls, an avatar could only hear their voices (singing and laughing), not see them or talk to them. I also don’t like that they showed the face of Eywa—it was better when they left it to us to imagine her in our own way. (This version of Eywa looked like a poorly edited AI video.) I also don’t like that they made Kiri special, only for her to end up needing the help of a human and a 9-year-old child to reach Eywa. (So how is she special then?) And I don’t like her flying braid that she used to attack Varang—yes, it looked powerful, but not for this movie.

Overall, I sat there for three hours and watched something I’ve already seen before. They showed a lot, but in the end, they showed nothing.

This is a comment from a longtime fan who has loved Avatar since the very beginning. 💙

I’ve finally seen the latest installment of Avatar. I haven’t watched the critical video yet, but I wanted to share my personal impressions. Having seen the entire saga at least once, I still wonder whether I’ll ever rewatch the third film. What is certain is that revisiting The Abyss by James Cameron gave me a familiar feeling: it feels like all the themes he has already explored are resurfacing here. Even though I know he wrote the entire saga and shot this latest film at the same time as the second one, one might wonder whether the second film could simply have been split into two parts rather than giving birth to a new installment.

From a technical standpoint, the film is undeniably impressive. It surpasses most productions of the year in terms of visual achievement and stands as a true technological feat. Paradoxically, however, what had amazed me in the second film—the aquatic environment—did not impress me as much this time. The novelty effect has worn off, and the story felt secondary compared to the visual experience.

That said, I did enjoy certain aspects, particularly the almost childlike pleasure of recognizing Sigourney Weaver’s voice portraying a younger version of her character. It reminded me that cinema is also a game of memory and references, a dialogue between past and present. Yet this time, the film felt more theatrical, as if each scene were a stage act rather than part of a fluid cinematic narrative. And when you think about it, the Avatar saga itself fits squarely within the tradition of American westerns: the clash of cultures, the fight for territory, and the exploration of society’s margins. It is fascinating to see how these stories, though rooted in a brief and often misunderstood historical period, continue to resonate in a universal context.

On a philosophical level, what Cameron proposes remains relevant: the defense of the environment, the confrontation between humanity and nature, and the way intercultural relationships are portrayed. This timeless message transcends eras and locations. However, I must admit that I did not grow attached to the characters. The fact that the human hero is paraplegic is something I had completely forgotten, and the integration of elements from the second film into this one makes the narrative feel overly long and, at times, disjointed.

Overall, it’s a film that can be appreciated for its spectacle and its message, but one that leaves a strong sense of déjà vu. Cameron once again proves that he can create fascinating worlds, but he also reminds us that technical grandeur alone is not always enough to renew emotion. If this third installment truly closes the trilogy, it mainly invites reflection on the repetition of themes and on our relationship with time, memory, and history.

To put it more simply, I really disliked the way humans are portrayed in the film, particularly Spider and Colonel Miles Quaritch. The way they speak sounds completely artificial, and this undermines the credibility of the inhabitants of Pandora as a whole. For example, when the teenagers talk among themselves, it feels like watching a B-movie. That’s when I realize that, despite the visual beauty and the fascinating sci-fi elements of Avatar, the screenplay and the characters’ narrative arcs remain very conventional and ultimately quite unoriginal.

EDIT: I watched the video, and after thinking it over, I still feel conflicted. You can say what you want, but there are no major spoilers, and in the end, not much actually happens. We’re mostly being asked to wait for Avatar 4, and that’s precisely what frustrates me.

In the second film, I was genuinely happy: they were in the water, exploring the planet, and there was a real sense of discovery. Here, we always talk about James Cameron, but we shouldn’t forget that he’s not alone—he has an incredible team behind him.

That said, I truly hope they release the films more quickly, because at some point, he’s going to age, and even 2030 still feels very far away. What can be criticized is that he created an extraordinary alien species and an extraordinary planet, yet doesn’t exploit them as much as he could, in my opinion. I sometimes feel like he’s remaking the same film over and over, with the same stakes, as is often the case throughout his filmography.

So yes, it’s still one of the biggest films of the year, but it may not be the one that moved me the most on a narrative level, especially for a Christmas release. I’m a generous audience member, and I admit I might be a bit demanding because I watch a lot of films throughout the year.

People will clearly turn out in massive numbers, but I also hear many saying that it feels repetitive. Some even claim it’s better than the second one. However, from a technological standpoint, nothing comes close to Avatar—that much is undeniable.
